:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 43,367 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 35% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

In Texas, the prison population includes two sizeable groups governed by different sets of general rules for parole release eligibility. The lower-severity offense group, a bare majority of inmates, are eligible for release on parole when their time served plus good conduct time equals one-fourth of the sentence imposed or 15 years, whichever is less. The higher-severity offense group, almost half of inmates, become eligible for parole release when they serve half of their judicial maximum sentences or 30 years, whichever is less, and without counting good time credits. Texas inmates may also be eligible for mandatory supervision release, and the Board shall release them, “when the actual calendar time the inmate has served plus any accrued good conduct time equals the term to which the inmate was sentenced.” However, the Board may deny mandatory supervision release to inmates if board members believe that “good conduct time is not an accurate reflection of the inmate’s potential for rehabilitation,” and that therefore “the inmate’s release would endanger public safety.”


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Hispanic, any race people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 36% (15,444 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 17,592 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 41% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
